channel.title: Nature Communications
channel.description:
Nature Communications is an open access, multidisciplinary journal dedicated to publishing high-quality research in all areas of the biological, health, physical, chemical and Earth sciences. Papers published by the journal aim to represent important advances of significance to specialists within each field.

We are committed to providing an efficient service for both authors and readers. Our team of independent editors make rapid and fair publication decisions. Prompt dissemination of accepted papers to a wide readership and beyond is achieved through a programme of continuous online publication.
channel.dc:rights: © 2026 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.
channel.prism:copyright: © 2026 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.

item.title: Small-molecule degraders for oncogenic KRAS<sup>G12C</sup> and pan-KRAS mutations
item.content:encoded:
Nature Communications, Published online: 26 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-71093-9

This work develops DJX-A-KM, a small-molecule KRASG12C degrader that recruits E3 ligase FBXO28 via dual covalent binding, inducing potent degradation. This strategy can possibly be extended to pan-KRAS degraders against a broader spectrum of KRAS mutations.
item.dc:title: Small-molecule degraders for oncogenic KRAS<sup>G12C</sup> and pan-KRAS mutations
item.dc:creator: Jianxiong Deng
Shujun Shen
Lei Huang
Fang Xu
Weizhen Huang
Chaoming Huang
Zhang Zhang
Tongzheng Liu
Yi Tan
Zhengqiu Li

item.title: Meta-analysis reveals widespread negative associations between species richness and ecological uniqueness
item.content:encoded:
Nature Communications, Published online: 26 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-70886-2

A fundamental question in ecology is whether species-rich communities tend to exhibit high ecological uniqueness. This study suggests that a negative association between species richness and ecological uniqueness is widespread across diverse taxa groups, and that the drivers of this relationship vary depending on whether species presence or abundance is considered.
item.dc:title: Meta-analysis reveals widespread negative associations between species richness and ecological uniqueness
item.dc:creator: Yue Chen
Janne Soininen
Jonathan A. Myers
Zikun Mao
Jinghua Yu
Xugao Wang

item.title: Leveraging weighted embedding and Transformer architecture to improve phenotype prediction of complex traits for crops
item.content:encoded:
Nature Communications, Published online: 26 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-71035-5

Available genomic prediction models lack scalability and interpretability. The authors introduce a deep learning framework that incorporates GWAS derived SNP weights into a hybrid CNN Transformer architecture, improving prediction accuracy and interpretability in soybean, maize, rice, and wheat.
item.dc:title: Leveraging weighted embedding and Transformer architecture to improve phenotype prediction of complex traits for crops
item.dc:creator: Jing Li
Linfeng Yu
Mengfan Li
Rui Han
Yecheng Li
Abdulwahab Saliu Shaibu
Kwadwo Gyapong Agyenim-Boateng
Zhaoyi Hao
Yitian Liu
Bin Li
Shengrui Zhang
Liang Li
Lijuan Qiu
Junming Sun

item.title: Extending the mean-field microkinetics for an accurate and efficient modeling of complex heterogeneous catalyst surfaces
item.content:encoded:
Nature Communications, Published online: 26 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-70896-0

The study presents a fast model that predicts catalyst nanoparticle performance while accounting for surface crowding and diffusion between facets. It matches detailed simulations at far lower cost, enabling rapid catalyst screening
item.dc:title: Extending the mean-field microkinetics for an accurate and efficient modeling of complex heterogeneous catalyst surfaces
item.dc:creator: Yaqing Wang
Tonghao Shen
Yuqi Yang
Xin Xu

item.title: Frequent floods in the Yangtze River basin linked to a shifted Indian Ocean wave regime
item.content:encoded:
Nature Communications, Published online: 26 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-70940-z

Enhanced quasi-biennial variability of East Asian Summer Monsoon rainfall over the Yangtze River Basin, along with more frequent floods in recent decades, is linked to intensified and faster baroclinic wave activity in the tropical Indian Ocean.
item.dc:title: Frequent floods in the Yangtze River basin linked to a shifted Indian Ocean wave regime
item.dc:creator: Panini Dasgupta
SungHyun Nam
Michael J. McPhaden
Dong-Jin Kang
J. S. Saranya
M. K. Roxy

item.title: Ceramide disrupts TM9SF2-PGK1 axis to redirect PD-L1 trafficking and enhance antitumor immunity
item.content:encoded:
Nature Communications, Published online: 26 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-70764-x

The PD-1/PD-L1 pathway is central to immunotherapy, but PD-L1 recycling limits durable responses. This study identifies TM9SF2 as a key regulator of PD-L1 trafficking and shows that disrupting its PGK1-dependent complex depletes PD-L1 and enhances antitumor immunity.
item.dc:title: Ceramide disrupts TM9SF2-PGK1 axis to redirect PD-L1 trafficking and enhance antitumor immunity
item.dc:creator: Yi Zheng
Fan Yang
Mengmeng Wang
Zhiying Wang
Xindan Zhang
Chenxin Huo
Yapeng Zhang

Shenghao Zhou
Luning Song
Wenpeng Jiang
Bowen Gu
Wei Zhao
Ting Dong

item.title: Bleb-based extravasation uses conserved morphodynamics but divergent calcium control
item.content:encoded:
Nature Communications, Published online: 26 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-71052-4

In birds, primordial germ cells exit blood vessels during development using bleb-driven movements similar to metastatic cancer cells. Comparison with cancer cells shows that this shared physical mechanism is controlled by distinct calcium signaling pathways across cell types.
item.dc:title: Bleb-based extravasation uses conserved morphodynamics but divergent calcium control
item.dc:creator: Mizuki Morita
Manami Morimoto
Junichi Ikenouchi
Bertrand Pain
Yuji Atsuta
Yoshiki Hayashi
Takayuki Teramoto
Daisuke Saito

item.title: Formation of S- and Z-twist supramolecular micro-ropes by peptide stereoisomers
item.content:encoded:
Nature Communications, Published online: 26 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-71043-5

The reconstruction and control of supramolecular ropes using minimalist building units remains a fundamental challenge. Here the authors demonstrate self-assembly of cyclo-tryptophan-proline dipeptide stereoisomers into crystalline supramolecular triple-helical structures with tunable S- or Z- twists governed by the configuration of tryptophan residues.
item.dc:title: Formation of S- and Z-twist supramolecular micro-ropes by peptide stereoisomers
